turns.content: I've been reading about BufReader for weeks, but I still don't get why we even need it. It just wraps a Read trait right? Why can't I just use raw syscalls directly in my Rust code?

The core reason is that raw syscalls are extremely inefficient because they trigger one system call per byte or small chunk of data. BufReader solves this by batching multiple read calls into fewer, larger ones, which drastically reduces the overhead from context switching between user space and kernel space. It wraps your underlying Read trait implementation to manage this buffering automatically without you needing to write low-level code.
Okay, so it's just a wrapper? But what if I want manual control over when data is actually read into my buffer? Is there an API for that?

Yes, BufReader provides methods like fill_buf and consume that let you access the internal buffer directly. However, these are primarily used in advanced scenarios where you need to implement custom logic or specific buffering strategies beyond what the default implementation offers.
That sounds complicated. I'm a Java dev coming from high-level abstractions; why would I want to mess with the internals? Can't I just stick to the public API?

For most applications, sticking to the public API is perfectly fine and recommended unless you're optimizing for extreme performance or debugging specific buffer issues. The public methods like lines() iterator or read_until handle common patterns automatically.
Alright, what about that lines() thing? Is it just a helper function or does it do something special?

The lines() iterator is clever because it handles newline detection and buffering internally. It buffers data until a complete line (ending with \n) is available, then yields the string. This avoids the need for you to manually track buffer state when parsing text.
Wait, so if I have a file with no newlines, lines() will just keep buffering forever? That sounds bad.

Exactly, and that's why read_until is often preferred for raw binary or line-based data without guaranteed delimiters. It lets you specify a delimiter explicitly and ensures the iterator stops when that delimiter appears in the buffer,
Got it. But how do I choose what size buffer BufReader uses? Is there a constructor parameter?

BufReader does allow you to set an initial capacity, but the default is usually sufficient for most use cases. The actual performance gain comes from how many syscalls are batched rather than just raw memory usage,
I'm still not convinced. I keep thinking about Java's BufferedReader which seems simpler and why do I need to worry about capacity or fill_buf at all?

Java's approach abstracts away the syscall batching, but in Rust systems programming you often have more direct control over resources. The trade-off is that manual buffering requires managing Read trait implementations yourself,
That makes sense, so I should just use BufReader by default? What if my data has weird encoding or no line breaks?
In those cases you would switch to using std::io::Read directly with a custom buffer strategy. You can implement your own buffering logic using the Read trait if needed,
Okay, but what about BufReader's capacity method? Does that actually matter for performance?

The capacity method returns the current allocated size of the internal buffer. While it doesn't directly control performance tuning, knowing this helps you debug memory leaks or unexpected allocations in your code,
I see. So I should just avoid manual fill_buf unless I really need to? And stick with BufReader for normal cases?

Precisely. For 95% of Rust projects, using BufReader with its standard methods is the right choice. You only dip into fill_buf or manual buffering when profiling shows you're hitting syscall overhead limits,
